one_line_takeaway: An empirical audit showing Google's SynthID-Text watermark survives lexical edits but is significantly weakened by paraphrasing and round-trip translation, plus SynGuard — a SIR+SynthID hybrid that restores F1 to >0.89 on three of four attacks (and ~0.78 on the hardest) at roughly SIR-level compute cost.
key_finding: SynthID-Text is robust to lexical edits but degrades sharply under semantic-preserving attacks: F1 falls from 1.0 (no attack) to 0.884 under synonym substitution (epsilon=0.7), 0.788 under copy-paste (ratio=10, with FPR spiking to 0.53), 0.842 under aggressive paraphrasing (lex=10,order=5, FPR 0.23), and 0.711 under Chinese round-trip translation (TPR 0.675, near random). SynGuard recovers most of this loss under identical attack parameters: substitution F1 0.884->0.965, copy-paste 0.788->0.891, paraphrasing 0.842->0.923, and back-translation (Chinese, NLLB) 0.711->0.777 with FPR cut from 0.225 to 0.07 — an average F1 gain the abstract states as 11.1% (the body variously reports 9.2%-13%). A secondary empirical claim: back-translation attack strength correlates with the translator's quality on the target language, not with intrinsic language-family distance, contradicting prior work [11]; under the stronger googletrans, SynGuard holds F1>=0.929 across all five pivots.
core_contribution: The paper makes two of its OWN contributions (distinct from the prior methods it builds on). First, it presents what the authors call the first comprehensive robustness evaluation of Google DeepMind's SynthID-Text watermark (prior work [3]) under four meaning-preserving attacks: WordNet+BERT-Large context-aware synonym substitution, copy-and-paste dilution, Dipper paraphrasing, and round-trip (back-)translation. Second, it proposes SynGuard, a hybrid watermarking scheme that fuses the Semantic Invariant Robust (SIR) algorithm (prior work [6]) with SynthID-Text's tournament-sampling mechanism. SynGuard generates three logit vectors per token (base LLM logits, SIR semantic logits, SynthID pseudorandom logits) and blends them as P = P_LLM + delta*P_SIR + (1-delta)*P_SynthID, with a matching detection score s = delta*(s_semantic+1)/2 + (1-delta)*s_gvalue. Note: SIR and SynthID-Text themselves are NOT this paper's inventions; the contribution is their integration plus the empirical attack study and two supporting theorems.
threat_model: The adversary receives watermarked AI-generated text and wants to strip its provenance signal (evade detection) while preserving the text's semantic meaning, WITHOUT access to the watermark secret key k or random seed. Four concrete attack primitives are studied: (1) synonym substitution up to a replacement ratio epsilon, (2) copy-and-paste dilution (embedding the watermarked span inside a larger block of unwatermarked human text at length ratios 5-30), (3) paraphrasing via Dipper with tunable lexical/order diversity, and (4) round-trip translation through a pivot language. A secondary forgery/spoofing threat is addressed by Theorem 2, which argues a non-watermarked text has exponentially small probability of exceeding the detection threshold because the keyed pseudorandom SynthID component behaves as uniform noise (~0.5) to any attacker lacking k.
limitations: (1) Scale: only ~1.3B-parameter backbones (Sheared-LLaMA-1.3B, OPT-1.3B) and 200/200 samples at T=200; no test on the production Gemini-scale models SynthID actually ships in. (2) The paper contains internal numerical inconsistencies that undercut confidence: SynthID copy-paste F1 at ratio=10 is reported as 0.788 in Table V but 0.821 in Table VIII; the lower bound of the headline improvement range is stated inconsistently as 9.3% (Sec VI-D) vs 9.2% (conclusion) — the abstract's 11.1% is an average and does sit inside that range, so it is not itself contradictory; and Sec VI-D describes the back-translation gain as F1 'rising from 0.777 to 0.711' which is written backwards (SynGuard 0.777 > SynthID 0.711). (3) SynGuard is not uniformly better — at low substitution ratio 0.3 its F1 (0.985) slightly trails SynthID (0.987), and it inherits SIR's ~2x compute cost (12.93 vs 6.09 s/it) and reliance on an extra semantic model. (4) Theorems 1-2 are informal (assumed Lipschitz constant, i.i.d. assumptions, no empirical calibration of tau). (5) SynGuard's own best-case back-translation numbers depend heavily on which translator is used (NLLB Chinese F1=0.777 vs googletrans Chinese F1=0.930), so robustness is partly an artifact of attacker tooling.

datasets_used: C4 (Colossal Clean Crawled Corpus), split into prompt + human-written reference segments
200 watermarked and 200 unwatermarked samples per experiment, fixed length T=200 tokens
quantitative_results: SynthID-Text under attack (Table V): no-attack F1=1.0; synonym substitution eps=0.7 F1=0.884 (TPR 0.82, FPR 0.035); copy-paste ratio=10 F1=0.788 (TPR 0.995, FPR 0.53); paraphrasing lex=10/order=5 F1=0.842 (FPR 0.23); re-translation Chinese F1=0.711 (TPR 0.675, FPR 0.225)

SynGuard vs SynthID under identical attacks (Table XI): substitution 0.965 vs 0.884; copy-paste ratio=10 0.891 vs 0.788; paraphrasing 0.923 vs 0.842; back-translation Chinese 0.777 vs 0.711 (SynGuard FPR 0.07 vs 0.225)
No-attack detection + runtime (Table VI): SynthID-Text F1=1.0 @6.09 s/it; SIR F1=0.9825 @12.50 s/it; SynGuard F1=0.9975 @12.93 s/it
Headline average F1 improvement of SynGuard over SynthID-Text: 11.1% (abstract); reported as 9.2%-13% in conclusion and 9.3%-13% in Sec VI-D
Ablation (Table XII): F1 rises with semantic weight delta — 0.0->1.0, 0.1->0.985, 0.3->0.990, 0.5->0.992, 0.7->0.995 (chosen), 1.0->0.983
SynGuard back-translation robustness by translator (Table X): NLLB Chinese F1=0.777 vs googletrans Chinese F1=0.930; googletrans holds F1 0.929-0.942 across German/French/Italian/Chinese/Japanese
SynthID re-translation by pivot (Table IV): French F1=0.738, Italian 0.813, Chinese 0.711, Japanese 0.819
baselines_compared: SynthID-Text (Dathathri et al., Nature 2024, ref [3]) — primary baseline audited and compared against
SIR / Semantic Invariant Robust watermark (Liu et al., ICLR 2024, ref [6]) — component method and detection/quality/runtime baseline
KGW green/red-list watermark (Kirchenbauer et al., ICML 2023, ref [1]) — discussed as prior lexical baseline (not re-run)
